name: Guava Lime Water Kefir
subtitle: Latin-inspired guava and lime probiotic soda — sweet, tart, and tropical
blrNote: Guava (peralu/amrood) is one of Bengaluru's most available and affordable fruits — ₹40–80/kg year-round at KR Market, Hopcoms, and pushcart vendors. Pink-fleshed guavas give the most beautiful colour. Limes (nimbu) are ubiquitous. This is one of the most budget-friendly water kefir flavours you can make in Bengaluru. At BLR temps, 24 hours of 2F is usually perfect.

IN.where: Homemade from water kefir grains. See water-kefir-basic recipe.
Available year-round at KR Market, Hopcoms, fruit vendors. ₹40–80/kg. Pink-fleshed variety preferred.

equipment.name: Plastic or nylon mesh strainer
equipment.notes: Fine mesh to strain out guava pulp and seeds.

equipment.name: Blender or fork
equipment.notes: For pureeing or mashing the guava.

tldr: Blend or mash ripe guava, add lime juice, combine with finished water kefir, seal and ferment 24–48 hours. A pink, tropical, budget-friendly probiotic soda.

steps.title: Prepare base water kefir
steps.instruction: Make a batch of plain water kefir following the water-kefir-basic recipe. Strain out the grains. You need about 750ml of finished plain water kefir.
tips: The 1F should be mildly tart and lightly sweet.
Reserve grains in fresh sugar water for your next batch.
steps.checkpoint: 750ml of strained, room-temperature plain water kefir

steps.title: Prepare guava-lime flavoring
steps.instruction: Wash and chop 1 medium ripe guava (about 120g) into small pieces. You can mash with a fork or briefly blend. Add the guava pieces/pulp, 20ml fresh lime juice, and optionally 1 tsp sugar to your swing-top bottle.
tips: Pink-fleshed guavas give a beautiful blush colour to the finished drink.
White-fleshed guavas work too — less colour but equally delicious.
Guava seeds are edible but hard — they'll be strained out later.
The lime brightens the guava's sweet muskiness. Don't skip it.
steps.checkpoint: Guava pulp and lime juice in the bottle

steps.title: Second ferment (2F)
steps.instruction: Pour the strained water kefir over the guava-lime mixture. Seal tightly. Place in a warm spot away from direct sunlight for 24–48 hours. Burp every 12 hours.
tips: Guava has moderate sugar content — carbonation builds at a steady pace.
With pink guava, the colour will be a beautiful soft pink.
The aroma will be incredibly tropical and musky — classic guava.
At BLR temps, 24 hours usually gives nice carbonation.
steps.checkpoint: Bottle pressurized, liquid is pink-tinged, aromatic

steps.title: Strain and serve
steps.instruction: Open carefully. Strain through a fine mesh strainer to remove guava pulp and seeds. Serve over ice with a lime wedge. Refrigerate for up to 5 days.
tips: The strained kefir may be slightly thicker than other flavours due to guava's pectin — this is normal.
A pinch of chaat masala on the rim of the glass makes this incredibly Indian.
Excellent over crushed ice as an afternoon refresher.
This is one of the cheapest water kefir flavours to make in India.
steps.checkpoint: Pink, tropical, fizzy guava-lime probiotic soda

culturalContext.story: Guava is beloved across Latin America and South Asia. In Mexico, where water kefir (tibicos) originates, guava agua fresca is a staple. In India, guava (amrood) is a daily fruit eaten with chaat masala at roadside carts. This recipe connects the Mexican origins of water kefir with India's abundant guava culture — a perfect cross-cultural fermentation.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Guava is native to Central America and was spread globally by Spanish and Portuguese colonizers. It found a second home in India, which is now the world's largest guava producer. The guava-lime combination is a classic across Latin America, known as guayaba con limón.
culturalContext.significance: This is the most budget-friendly water kefir flavour for Indian fermenters. Guava costs ₹40–80/kg in Bengaluru year-round, making this an accessible daily probiotic drink. It connects water kefir back to its Latin American roots while using a deeply Indian fruit.
culturalContext.funFact: India produces more guava than any other country — over 4 million tonnes per year. A single guava has 4 times the vitamin C of an orange, making your guava kefir a vitamin C powerhouse.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Guava thickens the kefir
thingsToAccountFor.description: Guava is high in pectin, which can make the finished kefir slightly thicker than other flavours. This is normal and pleasant — like a thin guava juice. Straining well helps.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Guava seeds are very hard
thingsToAccountFor.description: Always strain the finished kefir. Guava seeds are hard enough to crack a tooth if you're not expecting them. A fine mesh strainer catches them easily.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Not recommended for flavoured water kefir.
tips: Freeze into guava-lime popsicles for a tropical probiotic ice treat

variations.name: Guava Chili Kefir
variations.description: Add a thin slice of green chili or a pinch of Kashmiri chili powder. Sweet-heat combination inspired by Mexican street snacks.

variations.name: Guava Chaat Masala Kefir
variations.description: Add a pinch of chaat masala to the finished kefir just before serving. Tangy, spicy, salty — pure Indian street food vibes.
